title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

texts.A: [37]Investing billions in Mars missions while our own oceans remain largely unexplored and our climate collapses is a moral failure of the highest order.[/37] The 'backup planet' narrative is not only scientifically dubious but ethically dangerous; it encourages a disposable mindset toward Earth. [39]We should treat space as a laboratory for high-level physics, not a potential real estate market.[/39] The focus must remain on satellite technology that helps us manage terrestrial resources, rather than escapist fantasies of Martian colonies.
texts.B: The drive to explore is an innate human characteristic that cannot be suppressed by budgetary concerns. Historically, [37]every cent spent on space exploration has returned manifold in the form of technological spinoffs—from medical imaging to water purification.[/37] To view this as a zero-sum game between Earth and Mars is a fallacy. Furthermore, the commercialization of space is inevitable and should be embraced. Private companies will drive down costs, making the resources of the asteroid belt available to a resource-hungry humanity.
texts.C: [40]Space exploration is the ultimate insurance policy for the human race.[/40] While the concerns about Earth’s environment are valid, they should not preclude us from becoming a multi-planetary species. However, I disagree with the 'wild west' approach to space commerce. [38]We need a robust international legal framework to prevent space from becoming a battlefield for corporate monopolies.[/38] The moon and planets should be declared the common heritage of all mankind, protected from the kind of exploitation that has ravaged our own planet.
texts.D: [39]The current obsession with sending humans to Mars is more about national prestige than genuine scientific discovery. Robotic missions are cheaper, safer, and produce far more data.[/39] [40]The 'human element' adds immense cost and risk for very little scientific gain.[/40] We should prioritize the deployment of more sophisticated telescopes and automated probes. [38]I share the skepticism regarding the commercial exploitation of space; the logistical hurdles and astronomical costs make asteroid mining a pipe dream for the foreseeable future.[/38]

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the economic benefits of space exploration?
questions.explanation: B argues that every cent spent has returned 'manifold' benefits. A views the investment as a 'moral failure' and suggests the money should be spent on Earth instead.

questions.question: Which expert shares C's concern about the unregulated commercialization of space?
questions.explanation: C warns against 'corporate monopolies' and 'exploitation.' D also expresses 'skepticism regarding the commercial exploitation of space,' calling its goals a 'pipe dream.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar opinion to A regarding the purpose of space activity?
questions.explanation: A believes space should be a 'laboratory' for science, not a market. D also argues for 'scientific discovery' through robotic missions over prestige or commercial gains.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from D on the value of sending humans into space?
questions.explanation: D argues that 'the human element' adds cost with little gain. C argues that becoming a 'multi-planetary species' is the 'ultimate insurance policy' for humanity, justifying the human presence.
